content:
Hi all

(don't know if this is the right place, but it seems relevant from what I've seen)

Not too long ago I shared snippets of code adding some NAPI abstractions in, and I now think it could be worthwhile to work a bit more to share them with the community (sending patch sets, etc).

What do you think would be the best example to showcase usage of NAPI? I originally made these for a UDP tunneling driver, but like anything else in net, there is so much that needs to be done at once to get anything useful running (said UDP tunneling driver needed NAPI/GRO, RTNL, net core including socket buffer handling, udp sockets, udp tunnels - which other people have worked on as well - just for an ICMP echo to pass through and come back), and I don't know how best to focus my efforts on something worth any feedback.

It has seemed like a lot of abstraction-only patch series gave been getting responses from the C team more or less saying, paraphrased, "we're probably happy to take a look and bring this in, but we can't really provide any useful feedback without a real-world usecase so need to see that part first". So maybe it would be a good idea to reach out to netdev and see if they have any suggested projects that are (1) relatively simple (2) actually useful or needs to be done, i.e. will be merged if it proves functional (3) are independent enough from the rest of the system that they're open to having it in Rust

I'm kind of pulling this from the responses in https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/e3b4164a-5392-4209-99e5-560bf96df1df@lunn.ch/T/#t and one other such abstractions-only patch that I remember seeing
Ah, here is the other patch, same thing https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/657b43a4-6645-4afe-b60e-269624c1b9ae@lunn.ch/
So yeah - it seems like if you're interested, skip the examples and just run with a real project. Andrew's last response in that second chain give some ideas for how to do this

I am the person that sent the socket abstractions; I honestly don't get it: what am I supposed to provide? An example on how sockets can be used? I don't think it makes much sense.
My point is that Rust bindings are just wrappers for the C functionalities, not something new, so I don't understand what kind of "real-world use case" should be presented.
If the point is that since those abstractions are meant for modules, an example in samples/rust should be added showcasing the functionalities of the Rust API, then I agree; otherwise, I really don't understand.
Again, I am sorry if I might be saying something non-sense or imprecise but I am new to kernel and open source development.

Michele Dalle Rive said:

I am the person that sent the socket abstractions; I honestly don't get it: what am I supposed to provide? An example on how sockets can be used? I don't think it makes much sense.

I get (and share) your frustration despite also understanding the rule of not bloating the kernel with code that nobody really ends up using. Yet, I've also come to the conclusion that every single one of us who's worked on Rust networking has had to rewrite the proverbial wheel, and that unless we do something about it, this will keep going on, and people who would otherwise dig into Rust driver development will get discouraged by the sheer amount of work needed before they can even have something minimal working. That's why I think finding projects that netdev would approve is a good idea, so we can integrate a functional core of net and be done with it.

As for a PHY driver, I recall @FUJITA Tomonori saying he'd try and see if he could work on that. At the same time, I'm a random nobody with no strings to pull so I doubt I could get access to random hardware recent enough that it needs a new driver on Linux or that I could run it; we'll have to see how he fares. I only have my personal time and Rust knowledge to help.

I do wonder however, if netdev never accepts abstraction code that's not used by new (Rust) drivers, when will we have the opportunity to write code that will include abstractions for Ethernet and such? I think we're getting stuck in a loop of "I would rather have abstractions everyone agreed on to begin making drivers" and "We cannot merge abstractions without drivers already existing".
I got a machine that I can work on a PHY driver. I've just started. Hopefully, I can send something next month.
I think that new abstractions without a use case just exist as PRs in the rust-for-linux GH repo. Then they probably get sent at the same time as whatever it is that needs upstreaming

Amélie Gonzalez said:

I think we're getting stuck in a loop of "I would rather have abstractions everyone agreed on to begin making drivers" and "We cannot merge abstractions without drivers already existing".

I think that the abstractions do exist, they just hang out in the RFL repo instead of getting upstreamed unless they have a use case. Just because of course the kernel doesn't want dead code (even though that is unfortunately what a lot of Rust code is at this point...) and it's tough for maintainers to test the abstractions on their own (how can you know that they are doing the right thing if you can't debug or get performance numbers?).
I'm sure it doesn't hurt to send anything to the list for general feedback, but probably pays to make it clear that you're just looking for general feedback and not upstreaming on its own

I would honestly just reply to Andrew on that thread stating that you indeed don't have a use case but are looking for one, and are wondering if he has any suggestions. Or see if anyone around RFL is working on something that relies on it
FUJITA Tomonori Which phy out of curiosity?
Realtek
I know automotive phys were mentioned, is it something T1 by chance?
you meant TI PHY?
The protocol, as in 1000Base-T1/10GBase-T1
I assume not in that case, T1 just happens to be something I work with a lot on the hardware side

Oh, automotive industry invented a new ethernet protocol. I didn't know.

commit f3b766d981310989d524b076065a93b8f11bcab2
Author: Vladimir Oltean <vladimir.oltean@nxp.com>
Date: Tue Apr 18 22:01:41 2023 +0300

net: phy: add basic driver for NXP CBTX PHY

The above automotive for PHY is just 100BASE-TX.

Trevor Gross said:

Amélie Gonzalez said:

I think we're getting stuck in a loop of "I would rather have abstractions everyone agreed on to begin making drivers" and "We cannot merge abstractions without drivers already existing".

I think that the abstractions do exist, they just hang out in the RFL repo instead of getting upstreamed unless they have a use case. Just because of course the kernel doesn't want dead code (even though that is unfortunately what a lot of Rust code is at this point...) and it's tough for maintainers to test the abstractions on their own (how can you know that they are doing the right thing if you can't debug or get performance numbers?).

I talk about sockets rn, just because it is what i've been working on and what my knowledge is limited to; I ofc do not intend to be "more important" than any other new abstraction.

In my specific case, I've been working on sockets, which are literally the highest-level network interface and the simplest way for network communication; they are not necessarily targeted to drivers, but any module that needs some network support, since there is literally none, as of now. As I explained in the mailing list, my need for socket abstractions comes from a research on the feasibility of writing algorithms for distributed systems as a Rust LKM. Would providing such an example of LKM using sockets work as a "real world use case" or am I misunderstanding something? And how am I supposed to provide it? As a module in the samples/rust, as a link in the patch to the lkm or in another way? Btw, I really appreciate the time y'all are putting to help and explain.
Does LKM mean Linux kernel module?

If so, netdev probably also wants to know the purpose of the linux kernel module (i.e. what's the problem the module wants to resolve). This section of kernel development explains the necessarity of that. But of course, my understanding is that you are free to send out what are currently only useful to you and hope to find more attention. Surely it's better to let reviewers/maintainers know that, since otherwise they don't know how much effort they should put into the review process.

Yea with LKM i that. Thank u for your reply. I understand what you mean and it makes sense to me, if this was all about a new feature which ofc should be justified, to avoid useless code in the kernel.
Then I might have completely misunderstood the point of Rust for Linux. The Rust kernel documentation says:

Abstractions are not available for all the kernel internal APIs and concepts, but it is intended that coverage is expanded as time goes on. "Leaf" modules (e.g. drivers) should not use the C bindings directly. Instead, subsystems should provide as-safe-as-possible abstractions as needed.

Isn't the point to be able to provide the same APIs that exist in C, with the Rust safety features?
If so, why should an abstraction justify its existence? It exists just because the same API exists in C.
For example, I can imagine that the Rust synchronization support in rust/sync is meant to be used by modules; but how could you think of a real-world example of mutexes? they can be used for anything, it is up to the user. I thought the final goal was leaving freedom to the modules to be able to do in Rust whatever they could do in C.

I don't think you misunderstand anything. It's just a "as time goes on" thing ;-)

Rust support is still in the experimental stage: no one yet knows the scope of it, will Rust be used for all the drivers or just some? will Rust also be used for core kernel part (e.g. scheduler)? These are still questions required to answer. So it's natural to expect that people ask the hard question "why Rust???". Besides different people have various availability that they can work on Linux kernel, some may have extra time to spend on Rust and some may not (unless it's proven to be useful). So it might seem that Rust abstractions/bindings are treated differently in different subsystem (also some APIs are more general than others, e.g. locks vs sockets), but it's just the nature of decentralized open source projects.

Now I think the important question is "how we should react accordingly", a few thoughts of my own: although the merge (and the review from netdev) won't happen immediately, but as Trevor and I mentioned, nothing could prevent you from sending these bindings, you can keep improving it until the use cases are convincing enough. This will provide the abstractions for potential users and also persistently working on something is a good way to earn trust.

Boqun Feng said:

Now I think the important question is "how we should react accordingly", a few thoughts of my own: although the merge (and the review from netdev) won't happen immediately, but as Trevor and I mentioned, nothing could prevent you from sending these bindings, you can keep improving it until the use cases are convincing enough. This will provide the abstractions for potential users and also persistently working on something is a good way to earn trust.

I generally agree, though we might need to find a way to make it more obvious that pure-abstraction patchsets still need reviews (especially when marked as RFC and not intended to be merged), so that we're not asked to provide a module immediately alongside.

There's also the problem that leaving a lot of PRs developed by different people, all for their individual needs, at different times, with different understandings of the language, means that anyone who wants to pick them up is going to find the process of fitting it all together a bit clunky. Wouldn't it be better if we all worked on our abstractions together to make things coherent, and keep them in the RFL repository, progressively upstreaming the bits that are needed for such and such drivers?

Are you focusing on out-of-tree modules rather than something that is likely to be upstreamed? If so, definitely drop that specific term because it could be a source of confusion if they're thinking you are looking to support a future in-tree module. I think that generally there isn't a lot of code specifically to support OOT modules that isn't used by some IT mods (this is based on the notion that most things should be brought in-tree if they are to be maintained for >1 kernel version) but net may have exceptions since they are higher level. Just reply with the following:

  1. Definitely include something in samples/rust (you should just reply to the header email with the sample patch for now, but include it in your patchset if you send a v2)
  2. If your intended use case is out-of-tree, state that clearly and ask what their policy is for accepting OOT-specific things (and expect that they will likely say they don't accept them)
  3. Regardless of IT or OOT, link to any ongoing work you may have that uses this abstraction. Be clear about whether you eventually plan to try to get this work upstreamed
  4. Ask if they have any specific work that they are looking to get in-tree and would use these interfaces - even if you can't work on it, somebody else on RFL may be able to
  5. Respect that it may be a while before these abstractions are upstreamed. Yes - the long term goal is to have RFL work almost everywhere. But the way we get there isn't just by writing abstractions for everything into the kernel - it's by starting with core abstractions (which we have) and then slowly branching out with (hopefully) abstractions along with uses for them, and with support from the maintainers (who generally don't like maintaining dead or OOT-only code, even though some might be OK with it). Keep in mind that RFL still has a lot of winning over to do

And then open a PR with your bindings to the RFL repo if you haven't already, so others who want to use them can

Amélie Gonzalez said:

There's also the problem that leaving a lot of PRs developed by different people, all for their individual needs, at different times, with different understandings of the language, means that anyone who wants to pick them up is going to find the process of fitting it all together a bit clunky. Wouldn't it be better if we all worked on our abstractions together to make things coherent, and keep them in the RFL repository, progressively upstreaming the bits that are needed for such and such drivers?

I think this is the purpose of the rust-dev branch - but I'm not sure what our policy is for merging "done" things in
If it's any consolation, you made the papers :smile: https://www.phoronix.com/news/Linux-Rust-Sockets-Patches

Amélie Gonzalez said:

Boqun Feng said:

Now I think the important question is "how we should react accordingly", a few thoughts of my own: although the merge (and the review from netdev) won't happen immediately, but as Trevor and I mentioned, nothing could prevent you from sending these bindings, you can keep improving it until the use cases are convincing enough. This will provide the abstractions for potential users and also persistently working on something is a good way to earn trust.

I generally agree, though we might need to find a way to make it more obvious that pure-abstraction patchsets still need reviews (especially when marked as RFC and not intended to be merged), so that we're not asked to provide a module immediately alongside.

There's also the problem that leaving a lot of PRs developed by different people, all for their individual needs, at different times, with different understandings of the language, means that anyone who wants to pick them up is going to find the process of fitting it all together a bit clunky. Wouldn't it be better if we all worked on our abstractions together to make things coherent, and keep them in the RFL repository, progressively upstreaming the bits that are needed for such and such drivers?

It's definitely a good idea that we have a place to queue all the latest Rust network abstractions, i.e. making thing more organized for potential users. My idea is that

  • people can team up and review each other's patches
  • talk with Linux netdev maintainer that we can have a special tag for Rust abstraction patches, so that they know it's not urgent but we'd like the review process to happen in the mail list. For example [rust/net RFC] Add socket API.
  • figure out a way to queue all the latest abstraction (could be a branch or a list of patches), like linux-rt

I'm happy to help if you guys are interested in this or similar approach ;-)
One techincal point is that: I hope we can keep net as a separate crate, but I'm OK either way.

Trevor Gross said:

Are you focusing on out-of-tree modules rather than something that is likely to be upstreamed? If so, definitely drop that specific term because it could be a source of confusion if they're thinking you are looking to support a future in-tree module. I think that generally there isn't a lot of code specifically to support OOT modules that isn't used by some IT mods (this is based on the notion that most things should be brought in-tree if they are to be maintained for >1 kernel version) but net may have exceptions since they are higher level. Just reply with the following:

  1. Definitely include something in samples/rust (you should just reply to the header email with the sample patch for now, but include it in your patchset if you send a v2)
  2. If your intended use case is out-of-tree, state that clearly and ask what their policy is for accepting OOT-specific things (and expect that they will likely say they don't accept them)
  3. Regardless of IT or OOT, link to any ongoing work you may have that uses this abstraction. Be clear about whether you eventually plan to try to get this work upstreamed
  4. Ask if they have any specific work that they are looking to get in-tree and would use these interfaces - even if you can't work on it, somebody else on RFL may be able to
  5. Respect that it may be a while before these abstractions are upstreamed. Yes - the long term goal is to have RFL work almost everywhere. But the way we get there isn't just by writing abstractions for everything into the kernel - it's by starting with core abstractions (which we have) and then slowly branching out with (hopefully) abstractions along with uses for them, and with support from the maintainers (who generally don't like maintaining dead or OOT-only code, even though some might be OK with it). Keep in mind that RFL still has a lot of winning over to do

And then open a PR with your bindings to the RFL repo if you haven't already, so others who want to use them can

Thank you for your ideas and the list. Tomorrow I will reply to Andrew and I will basically say what you just said; I think I will also ask whether there is the possibility of still getting a feedback, just to know what can be improved.
Btw yea I have been focusing on OOT modules, since they basically have no API as of now. Sorry for not making it clear.

Boqun Feng said:

Amélie Gonzalez said:

Boqun Feng said:

Now I think the important question is "how we should react accordingly", a few thoughts of my own: although the merge (and the review from netdev) won't happen immediately, but as Trevor and I mentioned, nothing could prevent you from sending these bindings, you can keep improving it until the use cases are convincing enough. This will provide the abstractions for potential users and also persistently working on something is a good way to earn trust.

I generally agree, though we might need to find a way to make it more obvious that pure-abstraction patchsets still need reviews (especially when marked as RFC and not intended to be merged), so that we're not asked to provide a module immediately alongside.

There's also the problem that leaving a lot of PRs developed by different people, all for their individual needs, at different times, with different understandings of the language, means that anyone who wants to pick them up is going to find the process of fitting it all together a bit clunky. Wouldn't it be better if we all worked on our abstractions together to make things coherent, and keep them in the RFL repository, progressively upstreaming the bits that are needed for such and such drivers?

It's definitely a good idea that we have a place to queue all the latest Rust network abstractions, i.e. making thing more organized for potential users. My idea is that

  • people can team up and review each other's patches
  • talk with Linux netdev maintainer that we can have a special tag for Rust abstraction patches, so that they know it's not urgent but we'd like the review process to happen in the mail list. For example [rust/net RFC] Add socket API.
  • figure out a way to queue all the latest abstraction (could be a branch or a list of patches), like linux-rt

I'm happy to help if you guys are interested in this or similar approach ;-)

Sounds good to me; but wouldn't this be very similar to the original rust branch, and then only a part of it was actually upstreamed?

Michele Dalle Rive said:

Thank you for your ideas and the list. Tomorrow I will reply to Andrew and I will basically say what you just said; I think I will also ask whether there is the possibility of still getting a feedback, just to know what can be improved.
Btw yea I have been focusing on OOT modules, since they basically have no API as of now. Sorry for not making it clear.

Of course :smile: That all sounds good, I'm sure that they will be happy to help with a light functional review if they know it's not in the critical path (just make sure you do provide a sample before then, so they have something to compile & try)

I think that the rust branch was pretty experimentation heavy, just writing things quickish for testing but maybe not the best way for upstreaming. What we want now is something for patches that have some more thought, possible subsystem reviews, and will likely be brought in at some point (I thought that is what rust-dev is but I might be wrong here)

Trevor Gross said:

Michele Dalle Rive said:

Thank you for your ideas and the list. Tomorrow I will reply to Andrew and I will basically say what you just said; I think I will also ask whether there is the possibility of still getting a feedback, just to know what can be improved.
Btw yea I have been focusing on OOT modules, since they basically have no API as of now. Sorry for not making it clear.

Of course :smile: That all sounds good, I'm sure that they will be happy to help with a light functional review if they know it's not in the critical path (just make sure you do provide a sample before then, so they have something to compile & try)

Do you think I should send a v2, explaining better in the cover letter patch and attach the sample as a patch, or just add the sample as a reply with all the explaination

Trevor Gross said:

I think that the rust branch was pretty experimentation heavy, just writing things quickish for testing but maybe not the best way for upstreaming. What we want now is something for patches that have some more thought, possible subsystem reviews, and will likely be brought in at some point (I thought that is what rust-dev is but I might be wrong here)

Oh I see, sounds very good to me then :)
Sorry i accidentally created a topic out of my message lol

I don't think you need a v2 for now (especially since net may wind up in its own crate as Boqun said) - just reply to your existing [RFC PATCH 0/7] cover letter with a patch that adds sample(s), and then follow up with Andrew in the same chain that you currently have

Trevor Gross said:

I think that the rust branch was pretty experimentation heavy, just writing things quickish for testing but maybe not the best way for upstreaming. What we want now is something for patches that have some more thought, possible subsystem reviews, and will likely be brought in at some point (I thought that is what rust-dev is but I might be wrong here)

Right, lots of commits in rust haven't been posted in mail lists, and ideally, for net, we want to have a branch/list of patches that have been posted in the list, and get some reviews as a common base for people who are interested, that's different than rust branch.

rust-dev is another experiment, i.e. queuing patches which are almost ready, you can think it at a preview of rust-next branch. But surely we can extend its scope somehow. However, I think subsystem topic branches (e.g. rust-net-next or rust-net-dev) are better: since if we put all the experimental things together, someone needs to resolve conflicts and other maintenance, and we are going to do that again when patches go to rust-next, that's not really efficient.
rust-net-x sounds like a nice idea, espeicially since it would make it easier to keep net maintainers in the loop if they're interested
Also rust-dev is always based on rust-next while rust-net-dev may need to be based on a merge point of rust-next and net-next

Boqun Feng said:

Also rust-dev is always based on rust-next while rust-net-dev may need to be based on a merge point of rust-next and net-next

or just net-next
Can github have specific branches that are mirrors? I wouldn't mind seeing master and a few other important branches there too, just for reference and easier comparisons

Michele Dalle Rive said:

Thank you for your ideas and the list. Tomorrow I will reply to Andrew and I will basically say what you just said; I think I will also ask whether there is the possibility of still getting a feedback, just to know what can be improved.
Btw yea I have been focusing on OOT modules, since they basically have no API as of now. Sorry for not making it clear.

I want to emphasize that Rust-for-Linux follows the preference to in-tree code as the overall Linux project, so the abstraction/bindings only used by out-of-tree modules have very low priority on reviews. I know it sounds not fair since "we can do the out-of-tree development with C API but not Rust", however the abstraction/bindings that have no in-tree user are subject to remove if maintainers don't want to take the maintenance burden, so they are unlikely to get merged or even spent effort on.

Hi all, I work with @Michele Dalle Rive on sockets abstractions. Thanks a lot for the feedback!
Just to add a bit of context: our research group works on a prototype of a low-interference network service (link) implemented as a out-of-tree LKM. When we tried to "rustify" it we realised that main functionalities were missing, which led to the patch - meant to share useful abstractions with the community.

It's clear now why our use-case might be too weak for upstreaming , also because we cannot provide the full code sample as it is confidential at the moment. I do support the idea of having a dedicated light-reviewed rust-net-dev branch to group useful/fundamental networking abstractions that can eventually be used by a concrete use-case and upstreamed. It would also be a good place for starters and LKM devs like us: we personally did not know where to look when approaching Rust kernel networking. I expect future interns/researchers in our lab to work on this topic, it would be great to join efforts in an organised manner.

Davide Rovelli said:

When we tried to "rustify" it we realised that main functionalities were missing, which led to the patch - meant to share useful abstractions with the community.

The NAPI abstractions I shared (pending a hopefully wider release of the whole module and abstractions if/when our work on it gets published....) have that same goal. It's just useless to have people rewrite everything all the time.

Davide Rovelli said:

I do support the idea of having a dedicated light-reviewed rust-net-dev branch to group useful/fundamental networking abstractions that can eventually be used by a concrete use-case and upstreamed. It would also be a good place for starters and LKM devs like us: we personally did not know where to look when approaching Rust kernel networking. I expect future interns/researchers in our lab to work on this topic, it would be great to join efforts in an organised manner.

+1 on that too. As someone who was the intern in that scenario, I would have gotten a lot more done had I been able to use a coherent set of abstractions from the start, and knowing there was a place I could get feedback and potentially share what I added
A dedicated branch and review tag in RFL sounds like a plan. Doesn't look like any of RFL's net abstraction patchsets is getting reviewed anytime soon, let alone merged in mainline, anyways.
It's going to take time but I'm sure if we work together we can get all of that done, eventually :smile:

Amélie Gonzalez said:

A dedicated branch and review tag in RFL sounds like a plan. Doesn't look like any of RFL's net abstraction patchsets is getting reviewed anytime soon, let alone merged in mainline, anyways.

Indeed, it actually seems to have caused more problems than benefits. Our goal was to make some socket abstractions available but we ended up questioning the whole Linux development process, to the point that RFL team members had to jump in to say "don't mind them they are newbies" :D
I think we should be very careful when pushing Rust patches for networking and for other big subsystems I guess.
So yeah, yet another reason for the dedicated branch. If anyone has any suggestions on how to move forward for that, I'd be happy to help.

Also maybe it should be clarified somewhere in the website that the pure bindings should go somewhere in the repo and patches are exclusively for full-fledged in-tree use-cases.
Since RFL is a peculiar case, it seems logical at first to submit a patch for something that is in C (which must have already a use-case) but not in Rust

Indeed, it actually seems to have caused more problems than benefits. Our goal was to make some socket abstractions available but we ended up questioning the whole Linux development process, to the point that RFL team members had to jump in to say "don't mind them they are newbies" :D

I think we should be very careful when pushing Rust patches for networking and for other big subsystems I guess.

I don't think you did anything really "wrong", i.e. you were open on your intentions about the out-of-tree stuff. It is just that justifying code with out-of-tree users will not work (and it will put kernel maintainers on alert generally), and in this case, given it is Rust code, other kernel maintainers may have assumed the Rust subsystem was OK with this, and thus question the whole effort. This is why a clarification was needed.

So yeah, yet another reason for the dedicated branch. If anyone has any suggestions on how to move forward for that, I'd be happy to help.

We have been discussing this sort of dedicated branches (and other approaches) as we have been scaling things up when more people joined the effort. I would suggest that you and everybody interested in networking (@Davide Rovelli, @Michele Dalle Rive, @Amélie Gonzalez, @FUJITA Tomonori et al.) join one of our team meetings. Then we can decide how to proceed, and if it is a branch, who will take care of what, the criteria for patch acceptance, etc.

Also maybe it should be clarified somewhere in the website that the pure bindings should go somewhere in the repo and patches are exclusively for full-fledged in-tree use-cases.

To be clear, there is no such distinction, i.e. it is not about abstractions vs. full-fledged in-tree use cases.

The repository and the project is intended for in-tree kernel development. It is part of the kernel, e.g. it is in GitHub for convenience, but it could be just as well in git.kernel.org.

This means that, for instance, if we create a rust-net branch (and later perhaps rust-net-next etc.), the intention is to have a single place to collaborate easily on things that are eventually intended to go upstream in some form or another. It does not need to be perfect or final, of course, but it needs to be intended for upstreaming. This implies it cannot just be for out-of-tree purposes.

This does not mean we cannot have a branch for iteration or experimentation. But it means that, if at some point we decide something in the branch will not make it to upstream for whatever reason, we will remove it. That is, out-of-tree users cannot rely on the branch nor expect support from it -- same as for the in-tree kernel APIs.

It also means an experimentation branch on our side like rust-net should also have users in the branch. Exceptions can be made there for new abstractions while in development, but again, the idea is that the experimentation is targeted towards upstream.

Furthermore, sending abstractions to the mailing list is OK, and it is the intended procedure. However, the abstractions sent need to have a clear use case in-tree. Thus, when contacting kernel maintainers, it is important to clarify what that use case is (as well as justifying the use case itself, e.g. why it is worth the maintenance effort and so on), just like for any other patch that goes into the kernel.

I will add a few clarifications to the webpage and perhaps a new section on out-of-tree vs. in-tree.

Since RFL is a peculiar case, it seems logical at first to submit a patch for something that is in C (which must have already a use-case) but not in Rust

I am not sure I understand what you mean here. An internal API in C should have a use case as you say, but it also needs an actual user in-tree. That is why submitting the equivalent Rust API without a user is not enough even if the abstract use case may be clear. So one needs to send the first user too or there must be a clear and agreed user in the pipeline. But if the only users are out-of-tree modules, that is not enough. Much less so if those modules are not public.

Miguel Ojeda said:

We have been discussing this sort of dedicated branches (and other approaches) as we have been scaling things up when more people joined the effort. We have been discussing this sort of dedicated branches (and other approaches) as we have been scaling things up when more people joined the effort. I would suggest that you and everybody interested in networking (Davide Rovelli, Michele Dalle Rive, Amélie Gonzalez, FUJITA Tomonori et al.) join one of our team meetings. Sounds good. When are the meetings?

This means that, for instance, if we create a rust-net branch (and later perhaps rust-net-next etc.), the intention is to have a single place to collaborate easily on things that are eventually intended to go upstream in some form or another. It does not need to be perfect or final, of course, but it needs to be intended for upstreaming. This implies it cannot just be for out-of-tree purposes.

This does not mean we cannot have a branch for iteration or experimentation. But it means that, if at some point we decide something in the branch will not make it to upstream for whatever reason, we will remove it. That is, out-of-tree users cannot rely on the branch nor expect support from it -- same as for the in-tree kernel APIs.

It also means an experimentation branch on our side like rust-net should also have users in the branch. Exceptions can be made there for new abstractions while in development, but again, the idea is that the experimentation is targeted towards upstream.

OK, understood. I have been trying to explain that our purpose was never to push support for a OOT user in the kernel, but the opposite. The simplest thing to do for us would have been to keep everything in our own repo. We submitted a patch just to share simple abstractions which are part of our use-case with the sole purpose of making them available to other users.
The fact that patches must have an in-tree user makes sense but you will agree that not having basic building blocks or having them scattered across random repos will discourage new people from contributing. That's why I think it makes sense such efforts converging in a branch here. If it does not reach upstream, then no problem, we can stick to external repos

I will add a few clarifications to the webpage and perhaps a new section on out-of-tree vs. in-tree.

Thanks for that.

I am not sure I understand what you mean here. An internal API in C should have a use case as you say, but it also needs an actual user in-tree. That is why submitting the equivalent Rust API without a user is not enough even if the abstract use case may be clear. So one needs to send the first user too or there must be a clear and agreed user in the pipeline. But if the only users are out-of-tree modules, that is not enough. Much less so if those modules are not public.

I meant that if an API already exists in C in the kernel, it must currently have or at least have had an actual in-tree user. That's why submitting a patch with the Rust equivalent might make sense at first. And again, not trying to sneak in support for our LKM, just expose common building blocks for possible in-tree/out-of-tree uses.

I am not sure what you mean. Rust code is not really different from C code, i.e. same policies apply etc. Of course, sometimes exceptions may be made when justified, but that applies to both Rust and C sides as well.

https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing

In general, the kernel does not allow to integrate code without users, but exceptions can potentially be made for Rust code to simplify the upstreaming process early on.

FUJITA Tomonori said:

I meant that having a single branch for upstreaming that waits for real users would be simpler than multiple branch like rust-net, rust-crypto, rust-block, etc. Anyway, it's up to someone who maintain them.

I don't think the current plan is to have different branches for different subsystems. It's more a case-by-case approach, right now seems this is the best way to proceed for networking, so let's try it and see how it goes. Ideally, we don't need to have the branch or patch queue approach if the in-tree users are clear and strong or the subsystem maintainers are willing to take care of abstraction/bindings even though the real users don't exist for the moment.

FUJITA Tomonori said:

I meant that having a single branch for upstreaming that waits for real users would be simpler than multiple branch like rust-net, rust-crypto, rust-block, etc. Anyway, it's up to someone who maintain them.

There are several approaches that can work, yeah. It depends on the patch acceptance criteria, how many people is involved and whether they are willing to spend time reviewing patches for potentially any area of the kernel, etc.

Having separate branches (assuming we get more subsystems like rust-net where it would be needed) can be more tedious at times when one needs to cherry-pick bits from other branches, indeed.

On the other hand, the dependency chains are smaller/easier to grasp w.r.t. mainline, gives more flexibility (different branches may be at different stages, e.g. a group may be preparing the final branch for upstreaming), and may make it easier to establish a sub-subsystem later in mainline.

FUJITA Tomonori said:

I thought that exceptions could be more specific because we've sent Rust patches to some subsystems.

If you mean what constitutes an exception, that depends on the case. For instance, justifying an internal API with (only) an out-of-tree user is not going to work (because there is no in-tree user expected at all to begin with). But upstreaming some dependencies first so that it is easier to upstream the user later because it is already in the pipeline may be allowed.

In other words, the website says "to simplify the upstreaming process" because the paragraph is all about simplifying the process to upstream particular users and their dependencies, not about skipping the rule completely and putting code in that does not even have a user in upstreaming sight.
